coredata.dc:description: The constantly escalating pollution on the surface of cement-based materials affects architectural aesthetics and residents′ health, necessitating frequent cleaning and maintenance, which contradicts the concept of sustainable development. This study aimed to develop a novel photocatalytic cement material to enhance the self-cleaning capabilities of cement-based materials, thereby extending their service life and reducing maintenance costs. To achieve this, carbon quantum dots (CDs) and g-C3N4 (CN) nanocomposite photocatalysts (CDs@CN) were synthesized and integrated into white cement to prepare photocatalytic cements. The materials were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and ultraviolet–visible–near infrared (UV–Vis–NIR) spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance was evaluated by the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under xenon (Xe) lamp, Vis light and UV light. The CDs@CN nanocomposite displayed superior photocatalytic activity, particularly under Xe lamp irradiation, achieving over 95 % degradation of RhB within 1 h. Even under Vis and UV light, the photocatalytic cement retains robust self-cleaning capabilities, achieving pollutant degradation rates of 81 % and 63 %, respectively, within 1 h. This performance exceeded that of pristine CN and control samples, demonstrating effective light absorption and charge separation. The optimal photocatalyst concentration in cement was identified as 0.1∼5.0 wt%, balancing performance and dispersion. The developed CDs@CN-based photocatalytic cement material offers a sustainable solution for self-cleaning and environmental purification. This study provides insights into the design of functional cementitious materials and contributes to the sustainable development of the construction industry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The constantly escalating pollution on the surface of cement-based materials affects architectural aesthetics and residents′ health, necessitating frequent cleaning and maintenance, which contradicts the concept of sustainable development. This study aimed to develop a novel photocatalytic cement material to enhance the self-cleaning capabilities of cement-based materials, thereby extending their service life and reducing maintenance costs. To achieve this, carbon quantum dots (CDs) and g-C3N4 (CN) nanocomposite photocatalysts (CDs@CN) were synthesized and integrated into white cement to prepare photocatalytic cements. The materials were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and ultraviolet–visible–near infrared (UV–Vis–NIR) spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance was evaluated by the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under xenon (Xe) lamp, Vis light and UV light. The CDs@CN nanocomposite displayed superior photocatalytic activity, particularly under Xe lamp irradiation, achieving over 95 % degradation of RhB within 1 h. Even under Vis and UV light, the photocatalytic cement retains robust self-cleaning capabilities, achieving pollutant degradation rates of 81 % and 63 %, respectively, within 1 h. This performance exceeded that of pristine CN and control samples, demonstrating effective light absorption and charge separation. The optimal photocatalyst concentration in cement was identified as 0.1∼5.0 wt%, balancing performance and dispersion. The developed CDs@CN-based photocatalytic cement material offers a sustainable solution for self-cleaning and environmental purification. This study provides insights into the design of functional cementitious materials and contributes to the sustainable development of the construction industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the growth of industry and agriculture, sustainable development and the circular economy have become primary issues for most countries [1–3]. Cement-based materials, known for their high compressive strength, good durability, convenient production methods, and low cost, are among the most widely used building materials, including in house construction, bridge construction, and road paving [4–6]. However, the presence of multi-level pores and microcracks in hardened cement-based materials facilitates the entry of external chemical corrosive substances, significantly reducing the durability of cement-based structures [7–9]. Besides, global urbanization and industrialization continue to exacerbate indoor and outdoor pollution, leading to severe pollutant erosion on building facades. Substantial funds are required annually to clean building surfaces manually, aiming to slow down the deterioration of cement structures and enhance their durability. Consequently, developing cement-based materials with better durability and environmental friendliness has become a critical research focus [10–12]. Recent studies have provided new perspectives and solutions to address this issue by adding nanocomposites to cement. For instance, Abbas et al. [13] explored the use of industrial by-products to produce geopolymers, which not only reduce industrial waste but also improve the environmental performance of building materials. Abdelzaher et al. proposed an innovative approach by combining nanoplastic waste (NPW), ultrafine demolition waste (UDW) [14], and ultra-fine ceramic shards (CW) [15] with white cement (WC). This combination improves the mechanical properties of cement slurry and reduces its environmental impact. Some others [16–21] explore the application of nanomaterials in cementitious materials, particularly the use of nano-silica (NS) as a supplementary cementitious material (SCM) to enhance the mechanical properties, durability, microstructure, and rheological performance of concrete. The studies also investigate the synergistic effects of nano-silica with other materials such as nano-alumina (NA) and their potential applications in self-healing and improving chemical durability.

The role of adding functional nanocomposites to cement goes far beyond that. In a recent study, Abdelzaher et al. [22] explored the use of nano-titanium dioxide (TiO2) to enhance lime cement performance. By partially replacing cement/clinker, they found that TiO2 nanoparticles significantly improved the mechanical strength of cement paste, suggesting new directions for developing building materials with self-cleaning properties. Currently, TiO2 is widely used as the main photocatalyst in photocatalytic functional cement studies due to its good photocatalytic activity, non-toxicity, and low cost [23,24]. Photocatalytic technology, which absorbs solar light and causes valence band electrons to transition, generates photogenerated hole-electron pairs. These pairs undergo photochemical reactions with O2 and H2O on the surface, producing superoxide anion radicals and hydroxyl radicals with strong oxidizing abilities [25]. Utilizing photocatalytic materials, various organic pollutants can be oxidized and degraded into environmentally friendly inorganics such as CO2, H2O, and HNO3. Thus, photocatalytic technology holds great potential in environmental pollution treatment, sterilization, and mildew prevention [26]. Numerous studies have investigated the integration of photocatalytic materials with cement-based materials to impart self-cleaning, antibacterial, and adsorption purification properties, showing broad future application prospects in the building materials industry [27,28].

However, practical applications of photocatalytic functional cement face significant challenges: (1) The wide bandgap of TiO2 limits its response to visible (Vis) light, making it responsive only to ultraviolet (UV) light, which constitutes 5 % of sunlight; (2) The highly alkaline surface of cement can cause electron-hole recombination in TiO2, affecting its photocatalytic activity [29]. Therefore, it is essential to study Vis light photocatalysts with high alkali resistance for functional cement. Among the reported Vis-light-responsive photocatalytic materials, graphitic carbon nitride (CN) is particularly attractive due to its narrow band gap, good pH, and thermal stability as a graphene-like polymeric semiconductor [30,31]. Additionally, CN's layered nanostructure allows for stable and convenient integration with cement, unlike traditional semiconductors, and does not require complex device manufacturing [32]. However, CN still faces the common issue of narrow bandgap photocatalysts, where photogenerated carriers easily recombine. Constructing junctions has proven effective in promoting charge separation and improving photocatalytic performance by establishing appropriate band structures and new electron transfer pathways [33,34]. Most junctions are composed of inorganic semiconductors, which also suffer from pH stability issues. Thus, studying high alkaline-resistant materials for junction construction is crucial for developing and applying solar photocatalytic functional cement.

The main objective of this study is to develop a novel photocatalytic cement material that enhances the performance and application range of cement-based materials through the following key objectives: (1) developing efficient photocatalytic materials; (2) preparing self-cleaning functional cement; and (3) evaluating photocatalytic performance. In this work, the carbon quantum dots (CDs) and graphite carbon nitrides (CN) CDs@CN nanocomposite photocatalyst were first added to cement materials. This new material exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity and maintains stability in highly alkaline environments, crucial for the sustainable application of cement-based materials. Utilizing the unique optical and chemical properties of carbon quantum dots (CDs), we designed and prepared a metal-free CDs@CN based photocatalytic cement, which shows enhanced photocatalytic self-cleaning activity. Our approach employs a bottom-up strategy to thoroughly investigate both the intrinsic properties and performance of the photocatalyst and its nanocomposite with cement. Due to its distinctive band structure and resistance to free alkali metal and alkaline earth metal ions present in cement, the CDs@CN nanocomposite photocatalysts exhibit enhanced availability of photogenerated carriers and demonstrate outstanding photocatalytic degradation performance against Rhodamine B (RhB), a widely used but environmentally unfriendly organic dye. Our findings support the potential application of CDs@CN nanocomposite photocatalysts as additives endowed with self-cleaning functionality for cement materials. By improving the durability and self-cleaning performance of cement-based materials, this study helps extend the service life of buildings, reduce maintenance costs, and promote the sustainable development of building materials. The results of this study not only provide new ideas for developing photocatalytic cement but also offer references for researching other functional cement materials, promoting innovative development in the field of building materials.

The synthesis of CDs involved a hydrothermal reaction method. Specifically, 4.2 g of sodium citrate monohydrate was dissolved in 200 mL of deionized water within a 400 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. This solution underwent hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 5 h. Following the reaction, a transparent CDs aqueous solution was obtained through a dialysis process.

Bulk CN was synthesized by annealing 30.0 g of urea in a covered crucible (100 mL) at 520 °C with a temperature ramping speed of 25 °C/min in an air atmosphere. The yellow product was grinded, washed with deionized water, and dried. Then, CDs-modified CN were prepared via a rectification method. Typically, 0.6 g of CN was mixed with 25 mL of the CDs aqueous solution and 75 mL of deionized water, respectively. Each suspension was dispersed under sonication and rectified at 100 °C for 24 h to ensure the sufficient adsorption of CDs on to the CN surface. The final samples were washed with deionized water, dried, and grinded. The final samples were named as CDs@CN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of simulated cement pore solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The simulated cement pore solutions are prepared by adding KOH, K2SO4, Na2SO4, and CaSO4 in deionized water. The ions contained in the solution are listed in Table 1 . The pH of the solutions is adjusted to 12.89 ± 0.2, which is close to the pH of the cement pore solution.

The 0.1∼5.0 wt% CN or CDs@CN catalyst was added into the white cement dry powder (Beilida Co., Ltd) and mixed evenly. Then, 8 g of water was added into 20 g of the above mixture powder. After stirring evenly, the slurry was put it into a mold. The solidified photocatalytic cement samples were obtained after one day of storage at room temperature, which were named Cement-5%CN and Cement-5%CDs@CN, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of samples were obtained on a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2010, Japan). The morphology of samples was recorded on a ﬁeld emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, SU8010, Hitachi, Japan). The phase structures of samples were measured by an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan), employing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) with a scanning speed of 10°/min. FTIR spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer model frontier spectrometer (FTIR, Agilent Technologies, USA). The surface chemical environments were determined by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-5000, Ulvac-Phi, Japan). The absorption spectra were obtained using an UV–vis–NIR spectro-photometer (Cary 5000, Agilent Technologies, USA). The ﬂuorescence spectra were recorded by a ﬂuorescence spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon FL3-221, HORIBA, France). Transient photocurrent responses were obtained by the electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, ChenHua, China).

100 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of RhB aqueous solution with a concentration of 20 mg/L, and stirred for 1 h to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the light source and circulating cooling water were turned on to start the photocatalytic experiment. Then, 5 mL of solution was regularly centrifuged at high speed, and the upper layer of clarified solution was taken to test the absorption spectrum, and the characteristic absorption peak intensity of RhB at 553 nm was used as the basis for evaluating the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The full spectrum photocatalytic test uses a Xe lamp as the light source. The Vis light photocatalysis test is carried out by adding UVCUT-400 filter (λ > 400 nm) to the Xe lamp, while the UV light photocatalysis test is carried out using 365 nm LED as the light source.

For the analysis experiment of photocatalytic active species, isopropanol and benzoquinone were added as sacrificial agents for capturing ·OH and ·O2 − in the photocatalytic reaction system. The concentration of sacrificial agent is 0.01 mol/L, and the other processes are the same as above.

The photocatalytic performance test of cement samples was carried out according to the following process. 20 μL of 1 g/L of RhB solution was drop-wised to the surface of the sample and then the sample was kept in the dark for 1 h. Next, the samples were subjected to photocatalytic experiments under irradiation of Xe lamp, Vis light, and UV light. The diffuse reflectance spectrum of the sample is measured every half hour. The absorption peak intensity of RhB at 553 nm was used as the basis for evaluating the photocatalytic degradation efficiency.

As shown in Fig. S1a, the size distribution of the CDs is relatively uniform and the average size of the CDs is about 6 nm. The CDs prepared by hydrothermal method exhibited good hydrophilicity due to the abundance of hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface. Thus, they are distributed in a monolayer on the carbon film without agglomeration. In addition, the crystallization of CDs is relatively weak according to the inconspicuous lattice fringes (Fig. S1b), and the high-resolution TEM images (inset in Fig. S1b) show that the crystal plane spacing of CDs is 0.231 nm, which is close to the typical (100) crystal planes spacing [35]. Fig. S2a shows the Raman spectra of CDs. Under excitation of 514 nm laser, CDs exhibit a graphite G band near 1640 cm−1, while there are no D and 2D bands of carbon materials near 1350 and 2680 cm−1, respectively. This indicates that the carbon atoms of CDs are mainly sp
2 hybrid, with almost no sp
3 hybrid carbon atoms [36,37]. In the infrared spectrum of CDs (Fig. S2b), a stretching vibration peak of OH appeared at 3420 cm−1, a stretching vibration peak of C=O appeared at 1703 and 1635 cm−1, and a stretching vibration peak of C-O-C appeared at 1215 cm−1, indicating that the surface of CDs contains abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, confirming their good hydrophilicity [38,39]. The surface chemical environment of CDs was further analyzed by XPS testing. The XPS map of C1s (Fig. S3) shows that the binding energies of carbon atoms of CDs are located at 285.0, 286.7, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to the three structures of C=C, C−OH and COOH, respectively [40,41]. It has been calculated that the ratio of C−OH and COOH carbon atoms in the C1s map of CDs is 1.28. The percentage content of each atom in CDs is shown in Table S1, from which it can be seen that the surface of CDs is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, and no N atoms are detected on the surface of CDs, which proves the surface composition and structure of CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the aqueous solution of CDs is colorless, clear and transparent, and exhibits the characteristic absorption peak of CDs around 330 nm.

The peak positions of the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs tend to change with the change of the wavelength of the excitation light. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the fluorescence emission wavelength of CDs is redshifted continuously with the increase of excitation wavelength, and the intensity decreases significantly. Under the excitation of 300 nm and 350 nm UV light, the luminescence intensity of CDs is very high in the blue light range between 400 nm and 450 nm, indicating that CDs have good absorption and luminescence performance for UV light. Under excitation at wavelengths of 500 nm, 550 nm, 600 nm, and 800 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased, but no upconversion luminescence performance was detected. It is generally believed that in upconversion luminescence, excluding the negative effect of half-wavelength excitation light, the intensity of a single wavelength Xe lamp source is different from that of a laser light source with extremely high energy density, and it cannot cause upconversion luminescence through a multiphoton mechanism [42–44]. In some research work, it has been reported that CDs exhibit upconversion luminescence properties under Xe lamp excitation [45], but some reports refute the relevant results [46]. Such results are still up for debate whether CDs can generate upconversion phenomena under Xe lamp excitation. So far, the fluorescence emission mechanism of CDs is still unclear, and it is believed that the main factors that can affect the luminescence performance of CDs are particle size and surface chemical environment [47–49]. The surface of CDs is rich in non-radiative recombination centers such as carboxyl groups, which greatly affects the luminescence intensity of CDs.

Ordinary Portland cement is often in a high alkalinity environment with a pH value between 12 and 14, and the cement matrix contains a large number of free ions such as Ca2+, Na+, SO4 2− [50]. The influence of pH and the adsorption of impurity ions on surface will have a certain impact on the surface chemical environment of CDs. Based on the composition of the cement pore solution, the reagents in Table 1 were added to CDs, and their composition and pH were adjusted to be close to the simulated pore solution. In the above environment, the fluorescence peak positions of CDs did not change under different wavelength light excitation, but the overall emission spectrum intensity of CDs increased by more than 2 times (Fig. 1b). When ions are adsorbed on the surface of quantum dots, the fluorescence is enhanced mainly due to the following reasons [51,52]: (1) Surface state modulation: The adsorbed ions on the surface of quantum dots can alter their surface states, which are important factors affecting their fluorescence properties. The adsorbed ions may fill or passivate the defect states on the surface of quantum dots, reducing non radiative recombination and thereby improving fluorescence efficiency. (2) Energy transfer: Ions adsorbed on the surface of quantum dots may undergo energy transfer with the quantum dots, allowing the excited electron energy to be more effectively transferred to the luminescent center, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity. (3) Extended excited state lifetime: Ions adsorbed on the surface may prolong the excited state lifetime of quantum dots, giving more excited state electrons the opportunity to emit fluorescence through radiative recombination. (4) Recombination of electron hole pairs: The adsorbed ions may affect the recombination process of electron hole pairs inside quantum dots, making the recombination pathway more favorable for fluorescence emission. (5) Chemical environmental changes: Adsorbed ions may alter the chemical environment of quantum dots, thereby affecting their optical properties. For example, certain ions may introduce new luminescent centers or alter the energy level structure of existing luminescent centers. As for CDs, their surface is mainly composed of hydroxyl, carboxyl and other functional groups, with relatively strong negative charge. They have a strong adsorption effect on cations such as Ca2+, K+, and Na+ in simulated pore solutions, forming a tight positive ion coating layer on the surface, becoming the complex center of photogenerated electrons and enhancing the fluorescence emission intensity. The activity of photocatalysts is directly related to the concentration and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [53]. As shown in Fig. S5, under the irradiation of Xe lamp, Vis, and UV light, the photocurrent intensity of CDs decreases sequentially, indicating that CDs can generate the most photogenerated electrons under Xe lamp excitation. In addition, under the three excitation conditions, the photocurrent of CDs can instantly reach the maximum value in their environment, indicating that the photogenerated electrons generated by excitation can be instantly transferred to the surface. After 8 cycles, the photogenerated current intensity remained almost unchanged, indicating that CDs have relatively stable photogenerated electron-hole separation ability.

The efficiency curves of photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CDs under Xe lamp, Vis, and UV light irradiation are shown in Fig. 1c. It can be seen that under irradiation of Xe lamp, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CDs performs most efficiently. The performance under UV light irradiation is slightly inferior to that under Xe lamp irradiation, but far better than that under Vis light irradiation. The relative activities of CDs in photocatalytic degradation of RhB under different light sources excitation are consistent with the results of photogenerated current analysis, indicating that photogenerated electrons play a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CDs. CDs with excellent photocatalytic performance were selected to study their efficiency in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in a simulated pore solution of cement (Fig. 1d). The efficiency of the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CDs is only slightly degraded under Xe lamp irradiation. As mentioned above, various anions and cations in the simulated pore solution can be adsorbed on the surface of CDs as recombination centers of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which enhances the fluorescence emission spectral performance of CDs to a certain extent [51,52]. Correspondingly, the decrease in the number of photogenerated electron-hole pairs used to participate in the photocatalytic reaction does lead to a decrease in the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of RhB in simulated cement pore solutions, but this negative effect is not significant.

The photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CDs after excitation mainly occurs as shown in equation (1)－(4), and the strong oxidizing substances produced mainly include h+, ·O2 −, and ·OH [54].

As shown in Fig. S6, under the excitation of Xe lamp, benzoquinone and isopropanol are used as sacrificial agents for ·O2 −and ·OH [55]. The photocatalytic degradation of RhB in deionized water environment within 60 min was used as the reference data for normalized treatment. For the two reaction systems, the degradation efficiency of RhB by CDs decreased by 47.92 % and 14.38 %, respectively, after the introduction of benzoquinone and isopropanol. This shows that ·O2 − and photogenerated holes play a key role in photocatalytic reactions, which also proves the importance of photogenerated electrons in the reactions, while ·OH plays a secondary role in photocatalytic reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties and performance of CDs@CN composite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to overcome the weaknesses of CDs, it was combined with CN via a rectification method to enhance the light absorption and carrier separation of the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 2 a, after modification by CDs, the CDs@CN nanocomposite material maintains almost the same X-ray diffraction characteristic peak as that of pristine CN, indicating that the addition of CDs did not change the structure of CN which has a typical graphene-like carbon nitride structure [56]. The diffraction peak near 2θ = 13° corresponds to the periodic spacing between adjacent tri-s-triazine structures. The diffraction peak around 2θ = 27.4° corresponds to the distance between adjacent 2D layers. The modification of CDs also hardly altered the infrared absorption spectrum of CN, and did not exhibit the characteristic absorption band of CDs as shown in Fig. 2b. This is similar to the XRD results, indicating that the relative amount of CDs in the nanocomposite is so small that the infrared characteristic absorption peak of CDs is submerged in the stronger infrared characteristic absorption peak of CN. As a result, the FTIR spectra only show broad peaks in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 belonging to the typical stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H and a strong absorption band in the range of 1200–1600 cm−1 that is considered as the typical stretching vibrations of the C–N heterocycle [57]. The SEM and TEM images in Fig. S7 display the typical morphology of thin porous layers in CDs@CN nanocomposite materials. On the enlarged TEM image (Fig. 2c), pores with sizes up to tens of nanometers and CDs with sizes in the range of a few nanometers can be clearly observed. According to the nitrogen adsorption-desorption curves in Fig. S8, the specific surface area of CN and its nanocomposites with CDs reached 32.30 and 31.27 m2/g, respectively, which was mainly due to the porous structure. The activities of CN and CDs@CN photocatalysts were also evaluated and compared under irradiation of different light sources. Fig. 2d–f shows the efficiency curves of CDs@CN photocatalyst for degradation of RhB under Xe lamp, Vis light, and UV LED irradiation. The photocatalytic performance of CDs@CN was significantly better than that of CN under different light sources. In particular, CDs@CN can reduce the concentration of RhB solution to about 2 % of the initial concentration in only 10 min under Xe lamp light. In contrast, the concentration of RhB is still 48 % at this time for CN as the photocatalyst.

The CDs@CN photocatalytic performance under Xe lamps is better than that of Vis light, much better than that under UV irradiation, and it is also better than CN under light conditions. Xe lamps are a widely used light source that mimics sunlight and emits a spectrum close to natural light, including UV, Vis, and near-infrared (NIR). In the field of photocatalysis, the photocatalytic performance is usually related to the ability of photocatalysts to produce reactive oxygen species (such as hydroxyl radical ·OH, superoxide anion ·O2 −) under specific wavelength light irradiation, which can effectively degrade organic pollutants. CDs@CN exhibits better photocatalytic performance under Xe lighting, which may be caused by a combination of the following reasons: (1) Wide spectral absorption capacity: CDs@CN composite materials may have a wide range of light absorption, capable of absorbing the spectrum from UV light to Vis light and even near-infrared light. This means that it is able to utilize the wider spectral energy in Xe light for photocatalytic reactions. (2) Efficient electron-hole pair separation: In the photocatalytic process, the electron-hole pairs generated by photoexcitation need to be effectively separated for redox reactions. CDs@CN may have structural properties that favor electron-hole pair separation, which improves the photocatalytic efficiency. (3) Enhanced photostability: The photocatalyst may be affected by photocorrosion under Xe light. CDs@CN may have good photostability and be able to maintain its photocatalytic activity under prolonged light exposure. (4) Synergistic effect: The interaction between CDs and CN may have produced a synergistic effect, which improved the separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity of photogenerated charges. (5) Surface reactivity: The surface of the CDs@CN may have high reactivity, which is conducive to the adsorption and degradation of organic pollutants. (6) Thermal effect: Xe lamps illumination may generate a certain amount of heat, which helps to increase the temperature of the photocatalytic reaction, which may increase the reaction rate.

To gain insight into the intrinsic reason for the better performance of CDs@CN, the optical and optoelectronic properties of the photocatalysts were investigated in details. As mentioned above, CDs exhibit weak absorption of light with wavelengths greater than 300 nm (Fig. S4). There, in Fig. 3 a, the overall lower UV–vis absorption spectra for CDs@CN compared with CN may contributed by the slightly thinner thickness after reflow treatment [58]. When excited by photons with wavelength of 300 nm, the CDs@CN nanocomposite showed over 10 times higher fluorescence intensity than that of CN (Fig. 3b). In addition, the shape of fluorescence peak of CDs@CN is similar to pristine CN, but the peak position slightly shifts to a shorter wavelength which agree with the blueshift of absorption edge in Fig. 3a. This means the energy bandwidth of CN slightly increases and a large number of photogenerated electrons are injected into the conduction band of CN from CDs, constituting a type-I heterojunction [59,60]. The Linear sweep voltammetry curve of CN and CDs@CN are shown in Fig. S9. The obvious increase of the cathodic current of CDs@CN is an indication that the CDs on CN can promote the O2 reduction process in equation (2). Fig. S10 compare the photocurrent values of CN and CDs@CN photocatalysts under Xe lamp, Vis light, and UV light. It is foreseeable that the photocurrent signals generated by CDs@CN under irradiation of different light sources are significantly stronger than those of CN due to more photogenerated charge carriers in CDs@CN. In order to further explore the photocatalytic mechanism of CN and CDs@CN, benzoquinone and isopropanol were also introduced as sacrificial agents for ·O2 −and ·OH [55]. As shown in Fig. 3c, after the introduction of benzoquinone and isopropanol, the CDs@CN nanocomposite catalyst showed similar results to the CDs catalyst. It declares that ·O2 − and photogenerated holes play a key role in this photocatalytic reactions, which also proves the role of ·OH and photogenerated electrons in the reaction are dispensable. However, unlike CDs@CN nanocomposite catalyst, CN catalyst shows better photocatalytic performance after introduction of isopropanol. The addition of isopropanol fully consumes ·OH, thereby accelerating the reaction (3) and consuming more holes, avoiding its recombination with photogenerated electrons. In this case, reaction (1) also accelerates the generation of more ·O2 −, which plays a major role in the reaction of RhB degradation. Conclusively, ·O2 − plays a major role in the degradation of RhB for CN catalysts.

Through the above detailed analysis of the composition, structure, and photoelectric properties of nanomaterials CDs and CN to be used in photocatalytic cement, the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance can be further summarized. Fig. 3d displays the schematic diagram of photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CDs@CN nanocomposite catalysts. When the nanocomposite photocatalyst receives irradiation from sunlight, photons with wavelengths less than ∼420 nm will be absorbed by CN, while CDs can absorb photons with wavelengths less than ∼380 nm. The valence band electrons of CDs and CNs are excited to the conduction bands, respectively, to form electron-hole pairs. Since the two nanomaterials with compact interface form an energy band structure similar to that of type-I heterojunction, the electrons in conduction band of CDs at more negative potential levels will be transferred to the conduction bands of CN driven by the potential difference [59,60]. For the same reason, the photogenerated holes will also be accumulated in the valence band of CN. Whereafter, a part of the free electrons in the conduction band recombines with the holes in the valence band and emit photons. The other part of the electrons successfully migrate to the surface of CN, and reduce the adsorbed O2 to ·O2 − that can degrade RhB. In reaction (4), some RhB molecules adsorbed on the surface of CN can also undergo degradation pathway through direct oxidation by holes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CDs@CN based photocatalytic cement
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposite of CN based photocatalytic cement was prepared by mixing 5 wt%CN or CDs@CN into the white cement before the cement curing. Fig. 4 a displayed the XRD patterns of pristine cement, cement-5%CN and cement-5%CDs@CN. For pristine cement, it mainly consists of 3CaO·SiO2, 2CaO·SiO2, Ca(OH)2, and other traces of species, such as Na2O·4CaO·2MgO·4SiO2 [61]. After addition of 5 wt%CN or CDs@CN, the diffraction signals almost did not change. This result is due to the low amount of CN or CDs@CN added and the weak crystallinity of the polymer material. Meanwhile, the addition of an appropriate amount of photocatalyst did not significantly change the mineral composition produced during the cementing process of cement materials. The SEM images in Fig. 4b–d describe the microstructure of cement and its nanocomposite with CN and CDs@CN, respectively. For the pristine cement, many immature cluster hydration products appear in the fracture surface, and some morphological rod-shaped hydration products are disorderly stacked with loose and porous structure (Fig. 4b). The rough and porous surface structure of cement can provide a large number of loading site for photocatalysts [62,63]. The microscopic morphology of the nanocomposites did not change significantly after the addition of an appropriate amount of photocatalyst (Fig. 4c–d), which is consistent with the results of similar XRD patterns of cement and its composites with photocatalysts.

The photocatalytic performance of CN and CDs@CN based photocatalytic cement was displayed in Fig. 5 . No matter under the irradiation of Xe lamps, Vis light, or UV light, the performance of photocatalytic cement composite in degrading RhB is improved to varying degrees. On the whole, the photocatalytic activity increased with the increase of the amount of photocatalyst introduced. As shown in Fig. 5a and d, the sample of cement-5%CDs@CN can degrade RhB to less than 5.1 % of the initial concentration within 1 h under the irradiation of Xe lamps, while 8.7 % left for cement-5%CN and 23.1 % for pristine cement at the same condition. Moreover, when the light source was changed to Vis light (Fig. 5b and e), the photocatalytic performance of pristine cement decreased to 37.3 % left of RhB within 1 h due to the removal of UV light. However, the photocatalytic performance only shows a small amount of attenuation for cement-5%CN and cement-5%CDs@CN. Under irradiation of Vis light for 1 h, 8.5 % and 9.9 % of initial RhB concentration were left for cement-5%CN and cement-5%CDs@CN, respectively. This result means the contribution of UV light to cement-5%CDs@CN is higher than that of cement-5%CN. The photocatalytic degradation cures under irradiation of UV light (Fig. 5c and f) also confirms the above viewpoint. When UV light was used as the irradiation source, cement-5%CDs@CN exhibited significantly stronger photocatalytic performance compare to cement-5%CN and pristine cement. After irradiation for 1 h, only 19.2 % of RhB remains over cement-5%CDs@CN. Meanwhile, there are still 53.2 % and 44.3 % of RhB residue on the sample surface of pristine cement and cement-5%CN, respectively.

Adding photocatalytic materials to cement can improve its self-cleaning and environmental purification capabilities, but adding more is not necessarily better. The reasons include but are not limited to the following two points: (1) Saturation of photocatalytic efficiency: Within a certain range, the photocatalytic efficiency of a photocatalyst increases with the increase of its addition amount. However, when it reaches a certain saturation point, continuing to increase the addition amount will not significantly improve the performance, but may actually reduce the efficiency due to excessive aggregation of photocatalyst particles. (2) Dispersion issue: Photocatalysts need to be uniformly dispersed in the cement matrix to maximize contact area and light absorption efficiency. If the amount added is too high, it may cause the aggregation of photocatalyst particles, reduce the effective contact area, and decrease the photocatalytic effect. Therefore, the addition amount of photocatalytic materials needs to be optimized through statistical experiments to ensure that it does not have a negative impact on other properties of cement while improving photocatalytic performance, and is cost-effective.

This study conducts a thorough investigation into the development of novel photocatalytic cement materials, with a particular focus on the synthesis and application of CDs and CN nanocomposites. Building upon previous research, this study incorporates these nanocomposites into cement-based materials to enhance their self-cleaning capabilities and environmental performance. Key findings include the successful synthesis of CDs and CDs@CN nanocomposites, their integration into cement matrices, and the evaluation of their photocatalytic performance under various lighting conditions. The results indicate that CDs@CN nanocomposites exhibit superior photocatalytic activity compared to pristine CN, especially under Xe lamp irradiation, which simulates natural sunlight. This enhanced performance is attributed to the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the improved light absorption capabilities of the nanocomposites.

The research outcomes have significant implications for both theoretical understanding and practical applications in the field of sustainable building materials. Theoretically, this study enhances the understanding of photocatalytic mechanisms in cementitious materials. It particularly elucidates the role of nanocomposites in improving the separation of photogenerated carriers and their subsequent reactions to environmental pollutants. Practically, the successful integration of CDs@CN into cement-based materials marks a significant advancement. This integration paves the way for the development of self-cleaning and environmentally responsive construction materials. Such materials have the potential to reduce maintenance costs, extend the service life of buildings, and contribute to the sustainable development of the construction industry.

The research has identified several key lessons crucial for future studies and applications in this field. First, the importance of nanocomposite design is emphasized. The structure and composition of the CDs@CN nanocomposites play a critical role in their photocatalytic performance. This highlights the need for careful material design to optimize the photocatalytic properties of such composites. Second, the role of the light source is significant. The study found that Xe lamp irradiation was the most effective, but performance under visible light also showed promise. This indicates potential for energy-efficient applications. Third, there is a balance between photocatalyst concentration and performance. The research suggests that there is an optimal concentration of photocatalyst in cement-based materials. Beyond this concentration, performance does not improve and may even decline due to aggregation and reduced dispersion.

Despite the significant findings and contributions of this study, several limitations should be acknowledged for future research. First, the study primarily focused on the initial photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposites. Further research is needed to evaluate the long-term stability and durability of these materials under real-world conditions. Second, the synthesis and application of CDs@CN were conducted on a laboratory scale. The challenges and feasibility of scaling up the process for commercial production have not been addressed. Third, while the study highlighted the environmental benefits of the developed materials, a comprehensive assessment of their environmental and health impacts throughout their lifecycle is necessary. Finally, the photocatalytic performance was evaluated using Rhodamine B as a model pollutant. The effectiveness against a broader range of environmental pollutants and under different environmental conditions needs to be explored.

In conclusion, this research marks a significant advancement in the development of sustainable and self-cleaning cement-based materials. The findings lay a solid foundation for future studies. These studies can address the identified limitations and further optimize the performance of these materials for practical applications.
